Olten

SOSolothurn

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The bridge that wouldn't give up

Told from the perspective of the old wooden bridge itself: surviving floods, fires, and wars across 700 years, watching Olten grow from a tiny bridgehead into a railway city. The bridge has been destroyed and rebuilt so many times it has lost count — but it never stops connecting the two sides of the Aare.

Create This Story

The Alte Holzbrücke was first mentioned in 1295, making it one of the oldest documented bridge sites in Switzerland. Spanning 89 metres across the Aare, it has been repeatedly destroyed by floods, ice, and fire. The current structure dates to 1803. A severe fire in 1844 caused significant damage. It is one of the last historic covered wooden bridges still in daily use in Switzerland.

Kilometre Zero

A child discovers the memorial stone on track 12 and is transported back to 1856, when the first trains arrived and transformed a quiet town forever. They ride the first locomotive through the dark Hauenstein tunnel, feeling the mountain shake as Swiss engineering conquers the Jura for the first time.

Create This Story

Olten served as the provisional 'Kilometre Zero' for the Schweizerische Centralbahn network — a memorial stone on track 12 commemorates this. The Hauenstein Summit Tunnel (2,495 metres) was Switzerland's first railway tunnel, built 1853-1858 by English contractor Thomas Brassey. A shaft fire in May 1857 killed 52 workers and 11 rescuers. The tunnel opened 1 May 1858. The railway transformed Olten from about 1,000 people to 7,000 by 1900. Today Olten station handles 1,100 trains and 80,000 passengers daily.

The night the writers met

At the Bahnhofbuffet, the ghosts of Max Frisch and Friedrich Dürrenmatt are still arguing about literature. A child overhears them and must settle their debate before the last train departs. The station restaurant, where Switzerland's greatest writers once rebelled, becomes a stage for the most important argument in Swiss letters.

Create This Story

In 1971, prominent Swiss writers including Max Frisch, Friedrich Dürrenmatt, Peter Bichsel, and Adolf Muschg founded the Gruppe Olten at the Bahnhofbuffet — the station restaurant chosen as neutral, accessible ground. They seceded from the Swiss Writers' Union, championing political engagement in literature. At its peak the group comprised 22 established writers. It disbanded in 2002. The Schriftstellerweg in Olten today features over 70 listening stations with six audio tours narrated by Swiss authors.

The Stadtturm clock strikes thirteen

The 1577 clock face on the Stadtturm malfunctions at midnight, and each chime beyond twelve reveals a different century of Olten's history — Roman soldiers at the river crossing, Frohburg knights defending the bridge, striking workers filling the streets in 1918, and famous writers arguing in the station buffet.

Create This Story

The Stadtturm was originally built in the 13th century by the Frohburg counts. Rebuilt by Konrad Gibelin in 1521, it was raised between 1628 and 1676 to approximately 42 metres. Its clock face dates to 1577 and measures nearly four metres — one of the largest and oldest working public clocks in Switzerland. The tower has served as watchtower, prison, council chamber, and fire station. The oldest of its five bells dates to 1402.

250,000 voices

November 1918: a child in Olten watches Robert Grimm and the committee debate through the night. Should they call the general strike? The nine demands are written on a board — women's suffrage, the 48-hour work week, old-age insurance. History is being made in a railway station restaurant, and 250,000 workers across Switzerland await the decision.

Create This Story

On 4 February 1918, the Oltener Aktionskomitee was founded in Olten, chosen for its central rail connections. Chairman Robert Grimm led the committee's nine demands including women's suffrage, the 48-hour work week, and old-age insurance. On 10 November 1918, approximately 250,000 workers participated in Switzerland's largest labor action. The strike ended 14 November. The 48-hour work week was achieved in 1919; women's suffrage took until 1971.

The explorer from the Dreitannenstadt

Young Werner Munzinger leaves Olten for Africa, carrying a fir cone from the Born forest as a lucky charm. A story of adventure, curiosity, and homesickness that takes a small-town Swiss boy to the Horn of Africa, where he becomes a consul, explorer, and legend.

Create This Story

Werner Munzinger (1832-1875) was born in Olten. He served as French consul at Massawa, explored Eritrea and Sudan, and was killed in 1875 leading an Egyptian expedition near Aussa, Ethiopia. Alex Capus's novel Munzinger Pascha (1997) tells his story. Olten's nickname 'Dreitannenstadt' (City of Three Fir Trees) comes from its coat of arms showing three fir trees on a hill, representing the three Jura foothills — Born, Engelberg, and Säli — surrounding the town.

The three fir trees

Why does Olten's coat of arms show three fir trees? A child plants three seedlings on three hills and discovers each tree can whisper the story of the town it watches over — one speaks of Roman crossings, one of medieval markets, and one of the trains that made Olten the heart of Switzerland.

Create This Story

Olten sits in a valley formed by three Jura foothills: the Born, the Engelberg, and the Säli, earning it the nickname Dreitannenstadt. Its coat of arms shows three green fir trees on a hill. The area has been inhabited since the Magdalenian period (16,000-14,000 years ago). A Roman vicus existed at the site. First documented in 1201 as Oltun. The town's central position makes it equidistant from Zurich, Basel, Bern, and Lucerne — all reachable within 30 minutes by train.

Where Switzerland meets

A modern-day treasure hunt: clues hidden at six railway platforms lead a child across the station, the bridge, the Hexenturm, and the Stadtturm to discover why this small town became the heart of a nation. Every clue is a piece of Olten's story, and the final treasure is the view from the tower.

Create This Story

Olten's central position earned it the reputation as 'the place where Switzerland meets'. National organizations have traditionally met here because no delegation travels further than any other. The Bahnhofbuffet hosted three European rulers in 1893 — Emperor Franz Joseph I, Queen Victoria, and Emperor Wilhelm II. The Hexenturm (Witch Tower) dates to approximately 1420 and served as a prison for accused witches. The Spittelschür from 1415 was originally a hospital.
